common_name: Perennial Peanut (Pinto Peanut, Rhizoma Perennial Peanut, Ornamental Peanut)

planting.note: Plant sprigs (vegetative divisions) or sod plugs in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil; Arachis pintoi is one of the most popular and proven low-maintenance groundcovers for zone 8b-11, especially throughout Florida and the Gulf Coast where it has been widely used for decades as lawn alternative, median strip groundcover, and landscape cover. Spreads by rhizomes and stolons; mow occasionally to keep tidy. Drought-tolerant once established. Fixes nitrogen from the air, improving soil over time. The small yellow peanut-shaped flowers are decorative. Suppresses weeds effectively in established beds.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained, moderately fertile to lean soil — Perennial Peanut is one of the most adaptable and low-maintenance groundcovers for Gulf Coast zone 8b-11, establishing in a wide range of soil types and requiring minimal inputs once established. Arachis pintoi is a stoloniferous, mat-forming, perennial legume native to Brazil and Bolivia in the Fabaceae family, introduced to Florida and the Gulf Coast in the 1930s and now one of the most widely used ornamental groundcovers in the region. The plant produces a dense, attractive, low mat of 4-leaflet compound leaves (like a clover leaf) from creeping stolons and rhizomes, and throughout the warm months produces small (0.5-inch), bright golden-yellow, peanut-shaped flowers at the tips of short upright stems. As a legume, it fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule symbiosis with Rhizobium bacteria, improving soil fertility over time — making it doubly valuable as both an ornamental groundcover and a nitrogen-contributing cover crop when incorporated into garden beds. The plant spreads steadily but is not considered invasive. In zone 8b-9, established plants die back to the roots in hard freezes but regrow vigorously in spring. A true perennial in zone 9b-11, requiring no replanting. One of the finest groundcovers for hot, humid Gulf Coast landscapes. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Arachis pintoi (Perennial Peanut) for Florida landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Perennial Peanut groundcover for Gulf Coast zone 9
LSU AgCenter — Low-Maintenance Groundcovers for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Florida Cooperative Extension Service — Perennial Peanut as lawn alternative

common_name: Tree Marigold (Mexican Sunflower Tree, Wild Sunflower, Nitobe Chrysanthemum)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in full sun after last frost; Tithonia diversifolia grows extremely rapidly (up to 10 feet in one season) and requires space — do not plant near small shrubs or perennials it will overwhelm. In zone 9, it grows as a large, multi-stemmed perennial shrub; in zone 9b-10, it becomes a large, woody, tree-like shrub. The plant makes an outstanding green manure — cut and incorporate the nitrogen-rich foliage into garden beds to improve soil. Excellent for pollinators, butterflies, and hummingbirds. Self-seeds moderately. Prune hard after fall bloom to prevent becoming too massive.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil — Tree Marigold is one of the most fast-growing, adaptable, and drought-tolerant ornamental shrubs for zone 9-12 conditions and will establish vigorously in poor soil where many plants fail. Tithonia diversifolia is a large, fast-growing, woody-based perennial shrub native to Mexico and Central America in the Asteraceae family — not the annual marigold of the flower garden but a large, semi-woody to woody, perennial plant that can reach 10–15 feet in height in zone 9-12 and produces masses of bright orange-yellow, daisy-like flowers 3–4 inches across on the branch tips through fall and early winter. In zone 9, the plant cuts back to the ground in hard freezes and regrows vigorously from the root in spring; in zone 10-11, it forms a large, multi-stemmed shrub of impressive presence. The flowers are powerfully attractive to Monarch butterflies, Gulf Fritillaries, native bees, and hummingbirds — one of the most valuable late-season butterfly nectaring plants for zone 9-12 gardens. Beyond its ornamental value, T. diversifolia has attracted significant interest in organic agriculture as a 'biomass' green manure plant: the thick, succulent, nitrogen-rich foliage contains nutrients that, when cut and incorporated into the soil, release nitrogen and organic matter at rates comparable to commercial fertilizers. Used as a living mulch and soil-building green manure throughout tropical Africa. An outstanding, multipurpose native-range plant for zone 9-12 landscapes. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tithonia diversifolia for Florida landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tree Marigold as ornamental and green manure for zone 9
LSU AgCenter — Tithonia diversifolia: fast-growing ornamental for Gulf Coast Louisiana
ECHO Development Notes — Tithonia diversifolia as green manure and biomass plant

common_name: Spanish Needles (Beggar's Ticks, White Bidens, Shepherd's Needle)

planting.note: Spanish Needles establishes spontaneously throughout zone 8-12 in disturbed sunny areas and is more often managed than planted — most gardeners encounter it as a beneficial 'weed' rather than deliberately sowing it. Allow patches to self-establish in sunny, informal areas of the garden, wild garden edges, or meadow plantings. It is classified as native to the Caribbean and tropical Americas, naturalized broadly in the southeastern US. The barbed seeds (the 'needles') attach to clothing and animal fur — plant away from high-traffic areas to minimize this nuisance, but retain at garden margins for exceptional pollinator value. Non-toxic.

directions: Grows in full sun in any disturbed, well-drained soil — Spanish Needles is an opportunistic colonizer of open, sunny ground and once established in a garden, perpetuates itself by prodigious self-seeding, requiring only that it be tolerated in appropriate areas. Bidens alba is a fast-growing annual to short-lived perennial forb native to the Caribbean, Florida, and tropical America in the Asteraceae family, now naturalized and extremely common throughout the southeastern US, the Gulf Coast, the Pacific Islands, Asia, and tropical regions worldwide. The plant produces upright stems of opposite, dark-green, pinnately compound leaves and, from late spring through fall (essentially year-round in frost-free conditions), quantities of small white daisy flowers with 5–8 white ray petals surrounding a yellow disk center — modest in size (0.75–1 inch) but produced in remarkable abundance. The ecological value of Bidens alba as a pollinator plant in the southeastern US and Gulf Coast is genuinely extraordinary: it is documented as one of the single most important nectar and pollen sources for honey bees (Apis mellifera), native bees, hoverflies, and butterflies throughout Florida, where it is considered the most important honey bee nectar plant of the fall season. The small, barbed, twin-pointed seeds ('needles') attach easily to clothing and animal fur, explaining the spread of this plant. A keystone pollinator plant for Florida and Gulf Coast wild gardens. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bidens alba: native pollinator plant for Florida
Florida Native Plant Society — Bidens alba species profile and pollinator value
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Bidens alba as beneficial wildflower for Gulf Coast zone 9
Florida Department of Agriculture — Bidens alba as honey plant

common_name: Small-Flowered Campylocentrum (Leafless Florida Orchid, Ghost Orchid Relative)

planting.note: Mount on rough-barked wood or cork with exposed aerial roots in bright indirect to partial direct light; Campylocentrum micranthum is a leafless epiphytic orchid — like the related Ghost Orchid (Dendrophylax lindenii), it produces no leaves and obtains all photosynthesis through flat, green, chlorophyll-containing roots. The roots must receive light (bright indirect to partial direct) to support the plant. A very slow-growing, specialist orchid for patient collectors with appropriate high-humidity conditions. Wild plants are protected. Zone 10-12 outdoors. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow mounted on rough bark or cork in partial indirect to dappled direct light with high humidity — Small-Flowered Campylocentrum is a leafless, photosynthetic-root orchid that requires light on the roots and consistently warm, humid conditions to thrive. Campylocentrum micranthum is a minute, leafless, epiphytic orchid native to southern Florida (Collier and Miami-Dade counties), Puerto Rico, the Caribbean, Mexico, Central America, and South America in the Orchidaceae family, representing one of the fascinating group of 'ghost orchids' — orchids that have eliminated their leaves entirely and adopted a photosynthetic root system. The plant is visible only as a mass of flat, strap-like, green to gray-green roots spreading across the bark surface of its host tree. From these roots in summer and fall, tiny (2–3 mm) cream-white to yellowish flowers emerge on short racemes — the flowers microscopic by orchid standards, requiring a hand lens for appreciation, but formally complete with sepals, petals, lip, and column. Campylocentrum is a genus of approximately 60 species distributed from the Caribbean through tropical America, related to the more famous Dendrophylax genus that includes Florida's Ghost Orchid (D. lindenii). In south Florida, C. micranthum grows naturally on live oaks, pond apples, and other hardwood trees in hammock forests. A specialist's orchid of genuine ecological interest, particularly for collectors drawn to Florida's remarkable native orchid flora. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Campylocentrum micranthum: Florida native leafless orchid
Florida Native Plant Society — Campylocentrum micranthum species profile
American Orchid Society — Leafless Florida orchids: Campylocentrum, Harrisella, and Ghost Orchid
Florida Department of Environmental Protection — Native Orchids of Florida

common_name: Bahama Senna (Chapman's Senna, Mexican Senna, Coastal Senna)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in full sun in well-drained, sandy to rocky, alkaline to neutral soil; Senna mexicana var. chapmanii (Chapman's Senna) is the native Florida variety — found naturally in pine rocklands and coastal hammocks of Miami-Dade County and the Florida Keys. It is frost-sensitive and suited to zone 9b-12. Exceptionally drought and salt tolerant. The bright yellow flowers are a major nectar source and Senna species in general are the exclusive larval hosts for Cloudless Sulphur (Phoebis sennae) and Sleepy Orange (Eurema nicippe) butterfly caterpillars. Propagate from seed.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy, rocky, or alkaline soil — Bahama Senna is exceptionally drought and salt-tolerant once established and thrives in the challenging coastal and limestone rock conditions of South Florida where many other ornamental shrubs struggle. Senna mexicana is a compact, evergreen to semi-evergreen shrubby Senna native to the Caribbean (Bahamas, Cuba, Jamaica, Hispaniola) and southern Florida (Chapman's Senna / var. chapmanii, endemic to Miami-Dade County and the Keys) in the Fabaceae family. The plant produces pinnately compound leaves of 4–6 pairs of oval, gray-green leaflets and produces clusters of bright golden-yellow, five-petaled flowers at the branch tips through the warm months and intermittently year-round in zone 10-12. The flowers are followed by flattened, dark-brown seed pods. Bahama Senna is one of the most important butterfly host plants available for zone 9b-12 gardens: Senna species are the exclusive larval host plants for two of the most abundant and beautiful Florida butterflies — the large, brilliant yellow Cloudless Sulphur (Phoebis sennae) and the Sleepy Orange (Eurema nicippe) — both of which lay their eggs exclusively on Senna foliage and whose caterpillars feed on the leaves. A landscape with established Senna mexicana will be host to these butterflies throughout the warm months. An outstanding, low-maintenance native flowering shrub for coastal zone 9b-12 gardens. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Senna mexicana var. chapmanii for Florida landscapes
Florida Native Plant Society — Senna mexicana (Chapman's Senna) species profile
Florida Museum of Natural History — Cloudless Sulphur butterfly host plants
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Senna mexicana
